proof: [§319] In the case of resident aliens dealing with aliens, it shall be permitted them, as now, to give and receive oaths of a binding character one from another, if so they choose... and in respect of the private actions they bring against one another, they shall all have the same privilege during the trial.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While citizens are barred from oaths to prevent long-term spiritual/social pollution in the state, resident aliens are granted the right to use oaths in their private legal dealings with one another.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the category of non-citizens residing in the state.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement to outside countries
proof: [§320] First, no man under forty years old shall be permitted to go abroad to any place whatsoever; next, no man shall be permitted to go abroad in a private capacity, but in a public capacity permission shall be granted.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly forbids citizens under the age of forty from leaving the country, and forbids all citizens from traveling abroad for private reasons, restricting their freedom of movement.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects a very large segment of the population (all citizens under 40).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain personal property from state seizure
proof: [§319] in case of disobedience, those officers whom the State and the law appoint to exact the penalty shall take a pledge; and if any disregard the pledgings, the things pledged shall be sold, and the price shall go to the State.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law authorizes state officials to seize and sell the property of citizens who fail to comply with public duties or pay assessed fines.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to those who fail to meet civic obligations like festival attendance or war contributions.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as overseas inspector
proof: [§321] First, our overseas inspector shall be more than fifty years old; secondly, he shall have proved himself a man of high repute both in military and other affairs, if it is intended that he shall be despatched into other States with the approval of the Law-wardens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a strict age floor of fifty years for any citizen wishing to serve as an official overseas inspector, barring younger citizens from this specific political and diplomatic role.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a significant portion of the adult male citizen population who are below the age of fifty.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to act as inspector
proof: [§321] but when he has passed sixty years of age, he shall cease to act as inspector.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mandates an upper age limit, forcing citizens to retire from the role of inspector once they reach sixty.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This affects the subset of the population that has reached old age.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to attend the legislative synod
proof: [§321] None of these members shall go alone, but each of them shall bring with him a companion—a young man, selected by himself, between thirty and forty years old.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law explicitly grants young men within a specific age range (30-40) the right to participate in the high-level legislative synod as companions to senior members.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a narrow ten-year age bracket of the citizen body.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement within the city
proof: [§323] this stranger must be received, when he comes to the city, at the markets, harbors, and public buildings outside the city, by the officials in charge thereof; and they shall have a care lest any such strangers introduce any innovation, and they shall duly dispense justice to them, and shall hold such intercourse as is necessary with them, but to the least extent possible.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Trading strangers are restricted to specific peripheral areas (harbors, markets outside the city) and are denied full social interaction or movement into the city proper.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the broad class of visiting traders during the summer season.

extracted_rules.resource: Legal standing to have priest-judges
proof: [§323] And for these the priests shall act as judges, in case anyone injures one of them or one of them injures anyone else, if the claim does not exceed fifty drachmae; but if any greater claim is made, the trial for such strangers must take place before the market-stewards.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law provides a specific judicial mechanism for foreign observers ('inspectors with eyes and ears'), granting them the right to have priests adjudicate minor injuries or claims.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to a sub-category of foreigners visiting for cultural exhibitions.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from armed or concealed intrusion during search
proof: [§324] If anyone wishes to make a search on any man’s premises, he shall strip to his shirt and wear no girdle, and when he has first taken an oath by the appointed gods that of a truth he expects to find the object, he shall make his search
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law requires a searcher to be unarmed and nearly naked ('strip to his shirt and wear no girdle') to protect the householder from violence or the planting of evidence.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to any citizen whose house is subject to a legal search.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to permanent ownership through open use
proof: [§324] if the possessor be seen to be using it in the city, market, and temple, and if no one lays claim to it,—then if some man asserts that he has been looking for it all this time... and if this goes on for a year, the possessor still keeping the article... at the expiration of the year no one shall be allowed to lay claim to its possession.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law grants legal title to a possessor of an item if they use it openly for one year without a claim being made, effectively creating a right through prescription.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual involved in a property dispute over movables.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from forcible prevention of legal appearance
proof: [§325] If any man forcibly prevent any person from appearing at an action at law—whether it be the person himself or his witnesses,—in case that person be a slave of his own or of another man, the action shall be null and void; and in case the person so prevented be a free man, in addition to the annulment of the action, the offender shall be imprisoned for a year
extracted_rules.reasoning: While the prevention of a slave appearing in court nullifies the legal action, the law specifically denies the slave the additional protection afforded to free men: the person who prevents a free man is imprisoned, but no such criminal penalty is mentioned for preventing a slave.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the entire enslaved population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive private gifts for service
proof: [§325] Those who are performing any act of service to the State must do it without gifts; and it shall be no excuse nor laudable plea to argue that for good deeds a man ought to receive gifts... Perform no service for gifts. Whoso disobeys, if convicted by the court, shall be put to death once for all.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text strictly prohibits anyone serving the state from accepting gifts, under penalty of death, removing a common source of income/corruption for officials.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to any citizen currently performing an act of service to the state.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to appeal without financial penalty
proof: [§326] and if at the second trial the defendant is defeated, he shall pay as an extra penalty the fifth part of the assessed amount of the penalty recorded; and if, dissatisfied with his judges, he desires to fight his case before a court a third time, he shall bring it before the select judges, and if he be again worsted, he shall pay one and a half times the assessed amount.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato describes a system where seeking multiple trials increases the financial burden on the defendant if they lose, effectively penalizing the use of higher courts.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies to all individuals involved in litigation, which reflects a significant portion of the civic population but is limited to those in legal disputes.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to appeal without financial penalty
proof: [§326] Again, if the plaintiff, when worsted in the first court, does not rest satisfied, but goes to the second court... in case he loses, he shall pay the same fraction of the penalty. And if, through dissatisfaction with the previous verdict, they proceed to the third court... the prosecutor [shall pay] one-half of it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Standard Athenian-style legal theory (as presented by Plato) discouraged frivolous appeals by imposing financial losses on plaintiffs who failed to overturn a lower court's ruling.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any citizen acting as a prosecutor in the legal system.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to bring suit
proof: [§328] And if the means are not forthcoming, and there be a deficiency of not less than a drachma, the loser in question shall be precluded from suing anyone else until he has paid to the full his whole debt to the victor; but others may bring valid actions against him.
extracted_rules.reasoning: A person who has failed to pay a court-ordered judgment is legally barred from using the court system as a plaintiff, depriving them of legal standing until the debt is cleared.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to a subset of the population: those who have lost a lawsuit and remain in debt.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to use fertile land for burial
proof: [§328] no tombs, however, shall be put in places that are tilled,—whether the monument be small or great,—but they shall fill up those places where the soil is naturally fitted for this purpose only... but as regards all the places which of their own nature desire to produce food for mankind, of these no one, living or dead, shall deprive us who are alive.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law prohibits the use of arable land for funerary monuments to preserve food production for the living.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general regulation affecting all citizens and landholders regarding how they treat their property and deceased.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to spend personal wealth
proof: [§329] An expenditure on the whole funeral not exceeding five minas for a man of the highest property-class, three minas for one of the second class, two for one of the third, and one mina for one of the fourth class, shall be held to be moderate amounts.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Plato mandates specific sumptuary laws that limit how much a family can spend on burials based on their census class, with even the richest group having an absolute cap.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule categorizes the entire citizen body into four classes and applies specific financial limits to everyone.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of public expression
proof: [§330] we shall forbid loud mourning and lamentation outside the house, and we shall prohibit the carrying out of the dead on to the open roads and making lamentation while he is borne through the streets, and the funeral party must be outside the city-bounds before day-break.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law curtails the ability of families to express grief in the public sphere (roads and streets) and regulates the timing of funeral processions.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to every citizen and every household in the state.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to sit in the supreme governing synod
proof: [§331] The ten senior members, at the moment, of the body of Law-wardens shall form the synod, in company with all who have won the award of merit; and, moreover, those inspectors who have gone abroad to discover if they could hear of anything pertinent to the safekeeping of laws [...] shall, after undergoing a searching test, be deemed worthy to take part in the synod?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a specific senior governing body (the nocturnal synod) and restricts primary membership to the ten oldest Law-wardens and high-achieving inspectors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a very small, elite circle of high-ranking officials and elders within the state structure.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as political colleagues to senior magistrates
proof: [§331] In addition to these, every member must bring with him one of the young men, not less than thirty years old, whom he has first selected as being both by nature and training a suitable person; after selecting him, he shall introduce him among the members, and if they also approve, he shall keep him as a colleague.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While the synod is led by elders, men at least thirty years of age are granted a specific path to political participation as junior colleagues, provided they meet merit and selection criteria.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: It applies to a specific age cohort (30+) and requires individual selection and approval by a small council.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to participate in the governing synod
proof: [§331] In addition to these, every member must bring with him one of the young men, not less than thirty years old, whom he has first selected as being both by nature and training a suitable person.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This excludes a large portion of the adult and youth population based on a strict age threshold.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to higher education and dialectical training
proof: [§335] Should the State, do you think, have all its members equal instead of having some more highly trained and educated? Clin. Nay, my good sir, that were impossible. Ath. We must proceed, then, to expound a type of education that is higher than the one previously described.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly rejects educational equality, stating that the ruling class (wardens) must receive a 'higher' form of training not provided to the general citizenry.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This differentiates between the ruling elite (wardens) and the majority of the citizenry.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to serve as state observers (eyes of the state)

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This narrows the group from all 'young men' to only those who pass tests of intelligence and nimbleness of soul.

extracted_rules.resource: Eligibility for high magistracy
proof: [§336] Such exclusion from office consists in refusing ever to choose as a Law-warden, or to number among those approved for excellence, a man who is not divine himself, nor has spent any labor over things divine.
